nodes.name: Shakespeare
nodes.description: Shakespeare's plays: tragedies, comedies, histories, and romances

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy features a Danish prince who discovers his uncle murdered his father and married his mother?
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1600–1601; set in the Kingdom of Denmark
Characters include Hamlet, the ghost of his father, Claudius, Gertrude, Ophelia, Polonius, Laertes, and Horatio
'To be or not to be, that is the question' — the most famous line in English drama
Longest of Shakespeare's plays at ~30,000 words; Horatio alone survives the final bloodbath
'Alas, poor Yorick!' — Hamlet addresses a skull exhumed from a graveyard
Ophelia's madness and drowning is one of literature's most imitated female deaths
More productions, adaptations, and critical essays have been written about Hamlet than any other Shakespeare play
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Denmark; 'To be or not to be'; Ophelia, Yorick

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy features a Scottish general who murders King Duncan after hearing prophecies from three witches?
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1606; the shortest of Shakespeare's four great tragedies; set in Scotland
Characters: Macbeth, Lady Macbeth, the three Witches, Banquo, Duncan, and Macduff
Actors call it 'The Scottish Play' to avoid the curse tradition around saying its name backstage
'Out, damned spot!' — Lady Macbeth sleepwalks, obsessively washing imaginary blood from her hands
Macbeth is killed by Macduff, who was 'not of woman born' — delivered by Caesarean section
Written to flatter James I, who was Scottish and fascinated by witchcraft; James sponsored Shakespeare's company
'Double, double toil and trouble; / Fire burn and cauldron bubble' — the witches' chant
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Scotland; three witches; Lady Macbeth, Banquo

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy follows two young lovers from the rival Montague and Capulet families in Verona?
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1594–1596; one of Shakespeare's most frequently performed plays
Characters: Romeo Montague, Juliet Capulet, Mercutio, Tybalt, Friar Lawrence, the Nurse, and Paris
'What's in a name? That which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet' — Juliet on the balcony
Both protagonists die within minutes of each other after a failed communication involving a sleeping potion
Mercutio's dying curse — 'A plague on both your houses!' — is one of the most quoted lines
Adapted as *West Side Story* (1957), transplanting the feud to New York street gangs
Shakespeare drew the plot from Arthur Brooke's narrative poem *The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet* (1562)
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Verona; Montagues vs. Capulets; Mercutio, Tybalt, Friar Lawrence

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy features a Moorish general whose scheming ensign Iago convinces him his wife Desdemona is unfaithful?
alternates: The Tragedy of Othello, the Moor of Venice
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1603; set in Venice and Cyprus
Characters: Othello, Iago (one of Shakespeare's great villains), Desdemona, Cassio, Emilia, and Roderigo
Iago is widely considered the most purely malevolent villain in Shakespeare — he manipulates everyone for personal resentment
'The green-eyed monster' — coined the phrase for jealousy that is still in everyday use
Othello smothers Desdemona with a pillow after being deceived by Iago's planted handkerchief
Othello kills himself on learning the truth: 'I have done the state some service, and they know't'
The play is Shakespeare's most psychologically intimate: all destruction flows from Iago's manipulation, not fate or war
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Venice/Cyprus; Iago manipulates Othello; Desdemona, Cassio

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy features an aging king who divides his kingdom among his three daughters, then is betrayed by two of them?
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1605–1606; based on the legendary pre-Roman British king Leir
Characters: Lear, daughters Goneril, Regan, and Cordelia; the Fool; Gloucester; and his sons Edgar and Edmund
Cordelia refuses to flatter Lear and is disinherited; the loving daughter cast aside for honest words
Lear descends into madness on the heath during a storm; the Fool is his only honest companion
Cordelia is hanged on Edmund's orders; Lear dies of grief holding her body — one of the most devastating endings in literature
For 150 years after Shakespeare, a 'happy ending' version by Nahum Tate was the only version performed
Gloucester is blinded onstage in the most disturbing single scene in Shakespeare
items.cardFront: Tragedy; aged king; three daughters: Goneril, Regan, Cordelia; the Fool

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare comedy features the fairy king Oberon, queen Titania, and the mischievous sprite Puck in an enchanted forest near Athens?
items.explanation:
Comedy, written c. 1595–1596; among the most frequently performed Shakespeare plays
Characters: Oberon and Titania (fairy monarchs), Puck (Robin Goodfellow), four lovers (Hermia, Helena, Lysander, Demetrius), and Bottom's troupe of 'mechanicals'
Bottom is transformed with a donkey's head by Puck and becomes Titania's enchanted love object
'Lord, what fools these mortals be!' — Puck; 'The course of true love never did run smooth' — Lysander
The play-within-the-play 'Pyramus and Thisbe' parodies Romeo and Juliet (written around the same time)
Benjamin Britten's opera adaptation (1960) and Felix Mendelssohn's incidental music (1842) are landmark settings
Puck's closing monologue addresses the audience directly: 'If we shadows have offended...'
items.cardFront: Comedy; fairy forest; Oberon, Titania, Puck; Bottom with donkey's head

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play features the Jewish moneylender Shylock demanding a 'pound of flesh' from the merchant Antonio as collateral for a loan?
items.explanation:
Comedy (often classified as a 'problem play'), written c. 1596–1598; set in Venice and Belmont
Characters: Shylock, Antonio, Bassanio, Portia, Gratiano, Jessica (Shylock's daughter), and Nerissa
Portia disguises herself as the lawyer Balthasar and defeats Shylock: the bond permits flesh but not blood
'The quality of mercy is not strained' — Portia's speech is among Shakespeare's most quoted
'Hath not a Jew eyes?' — Shylock's plea for common humanity is a landmark in literary treatment of antisemitism
The casket test (gold, silver, lead) develops the theme of appearances versus reality
Modern productions typically play Shylock as a sympathetic or tragic figure; 19th-century actor Edmund Kean established this reading
items.cardFront: Comedy/problem play; Venice; Shylock's 'pound of flesh'; Portia in disguise as lawyer

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare comedy features the razor-tongued sparring between Beatrice and Benedick, who both deny they are in love?
items.explanation:
Comedy, written c. 1598–1599; set in Messina, Sicily
Characters: Beatrice, Benedick, Hero, Claudio, Don Pedro, the villain Don John, and the constable Dogberry
Beatrice and Benedick are each tricked by friends into believing the other is in love with them — the enemies-to-lovers trope
Hero is falsely accused of infidelity by the villainous Don John; she faints at the altar and is reported dead
Dogberry's malapropisms ('You are to be presented to the Prince's watch' for 'represent') are a running comedy
The phrase 'noting' (eavesdropping) was a pun on 'nothing' in Elizabethan English — the whole plot turns on overheard conversations
Kenneth Branagh's 1993 film adaptation starring Emma Thompson as Beatrice is the defining modern production
items.cardFront: Comedy; Messina; Beatrice and Benedick; Don John, Hero and Claudio

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare comedy features Viola, who disguises herself as the male servant Cesario and serves Duke Orsino, who courts the noblewoman Olivia?
alternates: Twelfth Night, or What You Will
items.explanation:
Comedy, written c. 1601–1602; set in the fictional duchy of Illyria
Characters: Viola (as Cesario), Orsino, Olivia, Sebastian (Viola's twin), Malvolio, Feste the Fool, Sir Toby Belch, and Sir Andrew Aguecheek
Malvolio is tricked into believing Olivia loves him; he appears in yellow cross-gartered stockings and behaves absurdly
'If music be the food of love, play on' — opening line, one of Shakespeare's most quoted
Feste's songs include 'O Mistress Mine' and 'When that I was and a little tiny boy'
Malvolio's humiliation borders on cruelty; some critics read him as the play's tragic victim
The title refers to the Feast of Epiphany (January 6), associated with festivity, misrule, and the end of Christmas
items.cardFront: Comedy; Illyria; Viola as Cesario; Malvolio, Feste, Sir Toby Belch

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare comedy features the famous 'All the world's a stage' speech and is largely set in the pastoral Forest of Arden?
items.explanation:
Comedy, written c. 1599–1600; set in a court and the pastoral Forest of Arden
Characters: Rosalind (disguised as Ganymede), Orlando, the melancholy Jaques, Celia, Touchstone the clown, and Duke Senior
'All the world's a stage, / And all the men and women merely players' — Jaques's 'Seven Ages of Man' speech
Rosalind, disguised as a man, teaches Orlando how to woo; considered Shakespeare's greatest comic heroine
'Sweet are the uses of adversity' and 'The fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows himself to be a fool'
Draws on Thomas Lodge's prose romance *Rosalynde* (1590) as its primary source
Unlike most Shakespeare comedies, it ends with four simultaneous weddings and the god Hymen presiding
items.cardFront: Comedy; Forest of Arden; Rosalind as Ganymede; Jaques's Seven Ages speech

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare comedy features Petruchio's aggressive courtship of the sharp-tongued Katherine Minola of Padua?
items.explanation:
Comedy, written c. 1590–1592; one of Shakespeare's earliest plays; set in Padua, Italy
Characters: Petruchio, Katherine ('Kate'), her compliant sister Bianca, their father Baptista, and Lucentio
Petruchio 'tames' Kate through sleep deprivation, denying food, and contradicting her perceptions of reality
Kate's final speech urging wifely submission is Shakespeare's most debated: sincere, ironic, or strategic survival?
Adapted as the musical *Kiss Me, Kate* (1948) and the 1999 film *10 Things I Hate About You*
Framed by a unique 'induction' scene with Christopher Sly, a tinker tricked into thinking he's a lord — the only such structural device in Shakespeare
Bianca's subplot involves multiple suitors disguising themselves as tutors to gain access to her
items.cardFront: Comedy; Padua; Petruchio woos Katherine; Bianca subplot

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy features the assassination of a Roman dictator by a conspiracy of senators, including his friend Brutus, with the line 'Et tu, Bruté?'
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1599; one of the first plays performed at the newly-built Globe Theatre
Characters: Julius Caesar, Brutus, Cassius, Mark Antony, Portia, and Calphurnia
'Et tu, Bruté?' — Caesar's dying words to his friend Brutus; unverified historically but famous theatrically
'Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me ears' — Antony's funeral oration is a masterclass in manipulative rhetoric
'The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves' — Cassius; inspired the title *The Fault in Our Stars*
Brutus is the tragic hero: a noble man undone by misplaced idealism ('the noblest Roman of them all' — Antony)
Caesar appears in fewer than half the scenes despite the title; Brutus is the actual protagonist
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Rome; Caesar assassinated; Brutus, Cassius, Mark Antony

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play — widely considered his final solo work — features the sorcerer Prospero stranded on a magical island with his daughter Miranda?
items.explanation:
Romance, written c. 1610–1611; considered Shakespeare's farewell to the stage
Characters: Prospero, Miranda, Ariel (spirit servant), Caliban (enslaved creature), Ferdinand, and Gonzalo
'We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep' — Prospero's central speech
Prospero is widely read as Shakespeare's self-portrait — a master of theatrical illusion who finally breaks his staff
Caliban has become a postcolonial symbol; 'Caliban' is an anagram of 'cannibal'
Ariel's song 'Full Fathom Five' is among the most beautiful verse in English
The island's setting was partly inspired by William Strachey's account of a 1609 shipwreck in Bermuda
items.cardFront: Romance (final play); magical island; Prospero, Miranda, Ariel, Caliban

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare history play features the outnumbered English victory at the Battle of Agincourt and the famous St. Crispin's Day speech?
items.explanation:
History, written c. 1599; concludes Shakespeare's second historical tetralogy
Characters: Henry V (formerly Prince Hal), Chorus, Fluellen, Princess Katherine of France, and the traitors Scroop, Cambridge, and Grey
'We few, we happy few, we band of brothers' — the St. Crispin's Day speech is one of the great rallying speeches in English
The real Battle of Agincourt (1415): ~8,000 English defeated an estimated 20,000–30,000 French; English longbowmen were decisive
'Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more' — from the siege of Harfleur
Kenneth Branagh's 1989 film made the St. Crispin's Day speech its definitive modern rendering
Henry courts Princess Katherine in halting French — a comedy scene showing the human side of the warrior king
items.cardFront: History; Agincourt (1415); 'band of brothers'; Fluellen

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare history opens with 'Now is the winter of our discontent' and follows the scheming Duke of Gloucester who murders his way to the English throne?
alternates: The Tragedy of King Richard the Third
items.explanation:
History and tragedy, written c. 1592–1593; covers 1471–1485, ending at the Battle of Bosworth Field
Characters: Richard (Duke of Gloucester / Richard III), Lady Anne, Buckingham, the young princes, and Queen Margaret
'Now is the winter of our discontent / Made glorious summer by this sun of York' — Richard's opening soliloquy
Richard orders the murder of the 'Princes in the Tower' — a historical controversy still debated by scholars
'A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!' — Richard's desperate cry at Bosworth where he is unhorsed and killed (1485)
Laurence Olivier's 1955 film and Ian McKellen's 1995 fascist-era adaptation are landmark productions
Shakespeare's Richard is heavily shaped by Tudor propaganda; the real Richard III is viewed more sympathetically by modern historians
items.cardFront: History/Tragedy; 'winter of our discontent'; villainous Richard; Bosworth Field

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy depicts the love affair between the Roman general Mark Antony and the Egyptian queen, ending in their mutual suicides?
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1606–1607; a sequel to *Julius Caesar* (Antony appears in both)
Characters: Mark Antony, Cleopatra, Octavius Caesar, Enobarbus, Octavia, Iras, and Charmian
'Age cannot wither her, nor custom stale / Her infinite variety' — Enobarbus's description of Cleopatra
Antony's infatuation with Cleopatra leads to defeat at the Battle of Actium (31 BC) against Octavius
Cleopatra kills herself by applying an asp (snake) to her breast after Antony's suicide, to avoid being paraded in Rome
Covers the vast canvas of Rome and Egypt in the final struggle between Antony and Octavius (who becomes Augustus)
Judi Dench, Helen Mirren, and Janet Suzman are among those who have played Cleopatra in landmark stage productions
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Rome vs. Egypt; Mark Antony and Cleopatra; Battle of Actium

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare romance contains the notorious stage direction 'Exit, pursued by a bear' and a sixteen-year gap between its two halves?
items.explanation:
Romance, written c. 1610–1611; one of four 'late romances' along with Pericles, Cymbeline, and The Tempest
Characters: Leontes (King of Sicilia), Hermione (his wife), Perdita, Polixenes, Florizel, and the rogue Autolycus
Leontes is overcome by sudden irrational jealousy, falsely accusing his wife Hermione of adultery
Hermione apparently dies but is revealed alive in the extraordinary final statue scene
'Exit, pursued by a bear' (Act III, scene 3) — the most famous stage direction in all of Shakespeare; Antigonus is killed offstage
'A sad tale's best for winter' — young Mamillius gives the play its title
The play pivots sharply from tragedy to pastoral comedy at the halfway point — a structural experiment unique in Shakespeare
items.cardFront: Romance; Sicilia/Bohemia; jealous Leontes; 'Exit, pursued by a bear'

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare 'problem play' features Duke Vincentio disguising himself to observe his corrupt deputy Angelo, who proposes a deal to the novice nun Isabella?
items.explanation:
Problem play (or dark comedy), written c. 1603–1604; set in Vienna
Characters: Duke Vincentio, Angelo (the corrupt deputy), Isabella (a novice nun), Claudio, Mariana, and Lucio
Angelo offers to spare Isabella's condemned brother if she sleeps with him — the play's central moral dilemma
The title comes from Matthew 7:2: 'With what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again'
'Some rise by sin, and some by virtue fall' — captures the play's bitter irony about justice and hypocrisy
Classified as a comedy but its tone is relentlessly dark; the 'happy' ending satisfies almost no one
Considered a 'problem play' because its themes of sexual coercion and institutional corruption resist comic resolution
items.cardFront: Problem play; Vienna; Angelo vs. Isabella; Duke in disguise

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy features the Roman general Caius Martius, who earns the name Coriolanus at the siege of Corioli, then is exiled by the Roman people?
items.explanation:
Tragedy, written c. 1607–1608; Shakespeare's most overtly political play; set in early Republican Rome
Characters: Coriolanus, his mother Volumnia, Menenius, the tribunes Sicinius and Brutus, and the Volscian general Aufidius
Coriolanus's contempt for the common people ('the many-headed multitude') leads to his banishment from Rome
He joins the Volsci and leads an attack on Rome, stopped only when his mother Volumnia pleads with him
'There is a world elsewhere' — his defiant line upon exile
Volunia shaped her son into a weapon for Rome's glory; she is one of the most powerful mother figures in all of Shakespeare
Ralph Fiennes directed and starred in a modern-dress film adaptation (2011) set in a contemporary Balkan conflict
items.cardFront: Tragedy; Rome; Coriolanus exiled; mother Volumnia, Aufidius

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare comedy features the portly knight Sir John Falstaff attempting to seduce two married women who conspire to humiliate him?
items.explanation:
Comedy, written c. 1597–1598; the only Shakespeare play set entirely in contemporary Elizabethan England (Windsor)
Characters: Falstaff, Mistress Ford, Mistress Page, Ford (the jealous husband), and the young lovers Anne Page and Fenton
Falstaff sends identical love letters to both women; they conspire to humiliate him twice — once hidden in a laundry basket dumped in the Thames, once disguised as a witch and beaten
The only play where Falstaff (the great comedy figure from *Henry IV*) is the romantic lead and primary butt of the joke
Tradition holds Queen Elizabeth I demanded Shakespeare write a play showing Falstaff in love, though this is unverified
Giuseppe Verdi's opera *Falstaff* (1893) is based on this play — his final opera, composed at age 79
Falstaff appears in *Henry IV* Parts 1 and 2 and is mentioned in *Henry V*; this is his only standalone vehicle
items.cardFront: Comedy; Windsor; Falstaff humiliated by Mistress Ford and Mistress Page
